title: From The Wreck
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Turn out, boys!ן¿½, ן¿½Whatן¿½s up with our super to-night?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The manן¿½s mad, Two hours to daybreak Iן¿½d swear,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Stark mad, why, there isnן¿½t a glimmer of light.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Take Bolingbroke, Alec, give Jack the young mare;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Look sharp. A large vessel lies jammן¿½d on the reef,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And many on board still, and some washן¿½d on shore.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Ride straight with the news, they may send some relief
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From the township; and we, we can do little more.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You, Alec, you know the near cuts; you can cross
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The Sugarloafן¿½ ford with a scramble, I think;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Donן¿½t spare the blood filly, nor yet the black horse;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Should the wind rise, God help them! the ship will soon sink.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Old Peterן¿½s away down the paddock, to drive
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The nags to the stockyard as fast as he can,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A life and death matter; so, lads, look alive.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Half-dressן¿½d, in the dark, to the stockyard we ran.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There was bridling with hurry, and saddling with haste,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Confusion and cursing for lack of a moon;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Be quick with these buckles, weן¿½ve no time to waste;ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Mind the mare, she can use her hind legs to some tune.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Make sure of the crossing-place; strike the old track,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Theyן¿½ve fenced off the new one; look out for the holes
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the wombat hills.ן¿½ ן¿½Down with the slip rails; stand back.ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½And ride, boys, the pair of you, ride for your souls.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the low branches heavily laden with dew,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In the long grasses spoiling with deadwood that day,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where the blackwood, the box, and the bastard oak grew,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Between the tall gum-trees we gallopן¿½d away,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We crashן¿½d through a brush fence, we splashן¿½d through a swamp,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We steered for the north near ן¿½The Eaglehawkן¿½s Nestן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We bore to the left, just beyond ן¿½The Red Campן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And round the black tea-tree belt wheelן¿½d to the west,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We crossן¿½d a low range sickly scented with musk
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From wattle-tree blossom, we skirted a marsh,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then the dawn faintly dappled with orange the dusk,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And pealן¿½d overhead the jayן¿½s laughter note harsh,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And shot the first sunstreak behind us, and soon
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The dim dewy uplands were dreamy with light;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And full on our left flashן¿½d ן¿½The Reedy Lagoonן¿½,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And sharply ן¿½The Sugarloafן¿½ rearן¿½d on our right.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A smothered curse broke through the bushmanן¿½s brown beard,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ He turnן¿½d in his saddle, his brick-colourן¿½d cheek
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Flushן¿½d feebly with sundawn, said, ן¿½Just what I fearן¿½d;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Last fortnightן¿½s late rainfall has flooded the creek.ן¿½

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Black Bolingbroke snorted, and stood on the brink
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ One instant, then deep in the dark sluggish swirl
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Plunged headlong. I saw the horse suddenly sink,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Till round the manן¿½s armpits the waves seemed to curl.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We followן¿½d,, one cold shock, and deeper we sank
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Than they did, and twice tried the landing in vain;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The third struggle won it; straight up the steep bank
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We staggerן¿½d, then out on the skirts of the plain.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The stockrider, Alec, at starting had got
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The lead, and had kept it throughout; ן¿½twas his boast
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That through thickest of scrub he could steer like a shot,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the black horse was counted the best on the coast.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The mare had been awkward enough in the dark,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She was eager and headstrong, and barely half broke;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She had had me too close to a big stringy-bark,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And had made a near thing of a crooked sheoak;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ But now on the open, lit up by the morn,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She flung the white foam-flakes from nostril to neck,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And chased him, I hatless, with shirt sleeves all torn
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (For he may ride ragged who rides from a wreck),
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And faster and faster across the wide heath
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We rode till we raced. Then I gave her her head,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And she, stretching out with the bit in her teeth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She caught him, outpaced him, and passed him, and led.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We neared the new fence, we were wide of the track;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I lookן¿½d right and left, she had never been tried
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At a stiff leap; ן¿½twas little he cared on the black.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Youן¿½re more than a mile from the gateway,ן¿½ he cried.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I hung to her head, touched her flank with the spurs
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ (In the red streak of rail not the ghost of a gap);
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She shortened her long stroke, she pricked her sharp ears,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She flung it behind her with hardly a rap,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I saw the post quiver where Bolingbroke struck,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And guessed that the pace we had come the last mile
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Had blown him a bit (he could jump like a buck).
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ We galloped more steadily then for a while.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The heath was soon passן¿½d, in the dim distance lay
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The mountain. The sun was just clearing the tips
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of the ranges to eastward. The mare, could she stay?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She was bred very nearly as clean as Eclipse;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She led, and as oft as he came to her side,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She took the bit free and untiring as yet;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her neck was arched double, her nostrils were wide,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And the tips of her tapering ears nearly met,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Youן¿½re lighter than I am,ן¿½ said Alec at last;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½The horse is dead beat and the mare isnן¿½t blown.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She must be a good one, ride on and ride fast,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ You know your way now.ן¿½ So I rode on alone.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Still galloping forward we passן¿½d the two flocks
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ At Mן¿½Intyreן¿½s hut and Mן¿½Allisterן¿½s hill,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She was galloping strong at the Warrigal Rocks,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ On the Wallaby Range she was galloping still,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And over the wasteland and under the wood,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By down and by dale, and by fell and by flat,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She gallopן¿½d, and here in the stirrups I stood
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To ease her, and there in the saddle I sat
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To steer her. We suddenly struck the red loam
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of the track near the troughs, then she reeled on the rise,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From her crest to her croup covered over with foam,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And blood-red her nostrils, and bloodshot her eyes,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A dip in the dell where the wattle fire bloomed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A bend round a bank that had shut out the view,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Large framed in the mild light the mountain had loomed,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With a tall, purple peak bursting out from the blue.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I pullן¿½d her together, I pressן¿½d her, and she
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Shot down the decline to the Companyן¿½s yard,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And on by the paddocks, yet under my knee
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I could feel her heart thumping the saddle-flaps hard.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Yet a mile and another, and now we were near
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The goal, and the fields and the farms flitted past;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And ן¿½twixt the two fences I turned with a cheer,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ For a green grass-fed mare ן¿½twas a far thing and fast;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And labourers, roused by her galloping hoofs,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Saw bare-headed rider and foam-sheeted steed;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And shone the white walls and the slate-coloured roofs
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Of the township. I steadied her then, I had need,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where stood the old chapel (where stands the new church,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Since chapels to churches have changed in that town).
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A short, sidelong stagger, a long, forward lurch,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A slight, choking sob, and the mare had gone down.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I slippן¿½d off the bridle, I slackenן¿½d the girth,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I ran on and left her and told them my news;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I saw her soon afterwards. What was she worth?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ How much for her hide? She had never worn shoes.
meaning: About: Adam Lindsay Gordon was an Australian poet, jockey and politician.
